title: Our app is slow and hard to maintain | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)

seo.title: Our app is slow and hard to maintain | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
seo.description: I help teams understand why a business application is slow and difficult to maintain, then shape a stabilisation path without defaulting to a rewrite.

page.title: Our app is slow and hard to maintain | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
page.h1: Our app is slow, fragile, and hard to maintain
page.description: I help teams understand why a business application is slow and difficult to maintain, then shape a stabilisation path without defaulting to a rewrite.
page.primaryQuery: our app is slow and hard to maintain

hero.title: Every change takes too long and performance is hurting the business
hero.subtitle: A common situation in older or overgrown applications where technical debt, weak architecture boundaries, and low operational confidence compound each other.

intro: A slow, fragile application is not only a technical problem. It shows up in team fatigue, slower delivery, more incidents, and weaker confidence in future investment.
The stronger first move is to identify what is actually slowing the product down and which part of the system needs stabilising first.

sections.title: Typical symptoms
body: If the situation is left alone, the cost is not only performance. Delivery slows down, operational trust falls, and the application becomes a business bottleneck rather than support.
bullets: slow load time or poor responsiveness in key workflows
large uncertainty around releases and change impact
weak visibility into performance and operational risk

sections.title: How I approach it
body: I start by mapping critical bottlenecks, release risks, and the parts of the system creating the most delivery drag. Only then does it make sense to decide between stabilisation, refactor, or larger architectural change.

sections.title: What a good outcome looks like
body: The goal is not a quick patch. The goal is to restore control, confidence, and a practical next step.
bullets: lower uncertainty around release and change
a stronger base for future delivery

faq.question: Is slowness automatically a reason to rewrite?
faq.answer: No. The first question is what is really causing the slowdown and how that affects the business and the delivery team.

faq.question: What if we lack good metrics or monitoring?
faq.answer: That is an important finding in itself. Part of stabilisation often includes improving visibility into the system.

faq.question: Can this be improved incrementally?
faq.answer: Yes. For most business applications that is the more practical and safer path.

for: running applications that are already dragging delivery down
teams under pressure on both performance and stability
products where technical debt is now a business problem
notFor: simple marketing sites with no real product workflows
teams unwilling to address operational root causes

cta.label: Explore the project fit
cta.note: A short summary of the current state and the main risk is enough to start.
